title: Try / Catch 🧯
description: How to save a burning program.

slides.title: The Safety Net 🕸️
slides.content:
To prevent an Exception from instantly destroying our program, we wrap the dangerous code inside a **`try`** block.

Java will 'try' to run the dangerous code. If it succeeds, great! But if it explodes, Java catches the explosion and cleanly hands it to the **`catch`** block instead of crashing.

slides.title: Try/Catch Syntax 🧯
slides.content:
Look at this structure. We 'try' to divide by zero. It instantly explodes. But we catch the `ArithmeticException` in the `catch` block and print a friendly message.

Notice how 'Calculations finished!' STILL PRINTS! The program survived!

quizData.question: What happens if the code inside a `try` block executes perfectly WITHOUT any errors?

options.text: It executes the `catch` block just to be safe.

quizData.explanation: The catch block ONLY activates if an explosion occurs. If the try block is safe, catch is ignored entirely.

slides.title: The 'e' Variable 📦
slides.content:
Did you notice `catch (ArithmeticException e)`?

The Exception itself is actually an Object. Java packages up all the data about the crash (what went wrong, what line number, etc.) into an object, and passes it into the catch block using the variable name `e` (short for error/exception).

slides.title: Printing the Error 🖨️
slides.content: You can print out `e.getMessage()` to read Java's official description of why it exploded, without actually letting it crash your app.

quizData.question: Why do we declare a variable `e` inside the catch parentheses? e.g. `catch(Exception e)`

options.text: Because it acts like a Parameter, capturing the 'Explosion Object' so we can inspect it.

quizData.explanation: Java creates an Exception object when it crashes. It throws it into the `e` variable so you can analyze it inside the catch block.
